conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first one: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

To assess eligibility for each child, I need to ask: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 20, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. So Alex is 20, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 5.

Now, let me ask about Alex first, as they're 20 years old. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they're in approved education or training)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex is 20 and not in approved education or training, they wouldn't qualify for Child Benefit.

Now let me ask about Blake, who is 16. **Is Blake in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake just left school recently. They signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Blake registered with the careers service after leaving education.

Now, regarding all three children, I need to ask: **Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie all live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes - I'm in Manchester. Alex and Charlie live with me, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So Alex and Charlie live with you in Manchester, but Blake doesn't live with you.

**Where does Blake live, and do you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep?** (For example, do you pay for things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contribute to their accommodation?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their dad. I'm not currently able to contribute financially to their upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. So Blake lives with their dad and you're not currently contributing financially.

Now let me ask about Charlie, who is 5 and lives with you. **Is Charlie in any special circumstances, such as being in hospital, care, or living away from home for any reason, or do they live with you full-time?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with me full-time, no special circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one more important question to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children - Alex, Blake, or Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake's dad claims Child Benefit for Blake since they live with him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. Let me also check: **Does anyone else claim Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else claims Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility for each of your children:

**Alex (20 years old): Not eligible**
Since Alex is 20 and not in approved education or training, they don't qualify for Child Benefit. The benefit only continues for children aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training.

**Blake (16 years old): Not eligible for you to claim**
Although Blake recently left education and registered with the careers service (which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks), Blake lives with their dad and you're not contributing financially to their upkeep. Since Blake's dad is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim for each child, you are not eligible to claim for Blake.

**Charlie (5 years old): Eligible ✓**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie is under 16, lives with you full-time in the UK, and nobody else is claiming for them.

So overall, **you can claim Child Benefit for Charlie**. If you haven't already claimed, you should apply as soon as possible. If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit only continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 and not in education or training, they do not qualify for Child Benefit.

Blake is 16 and recently left education but has registered with the careers service. While this would normally allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks, Blake does not live with you and you are not contributing financially to their upkeep. According to the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either you live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. Since neither condition is met, and Blake's dad is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake (only one person can claim for each child), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old, lives with you full-time in the UK, has no special circumstances, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. Since Charlie is under 16, you qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children. Alex (20) does not qualify as they are over the age limit and not in approved education or training. Blake (16) does not qualify for the claimant because Blake lives with their father who already claims Child Benefit, and the claimant does not contribute financially to Blake's upkeep. Charlie (5) does qualify as they are under 16, live with the claimant in the UK, and nobody else is claiming for them.

Since Alex is 20 and not in education or training, they do not qualify for Child Benefit.

Blake is 16 and recently left education but has registered with the careers service. While this would normally allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks, Blake does not live with you and you are not contributing financially to their upkeep. According to the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either you live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. Since neither condition is met, and Blake's dad is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake (only one person can claim for each child), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old, lives with you full-time in the UK, has no special circumstances, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. Since Charlie is under 16, you qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children. Alex (20) does not qualify as they are over the age limit and not in approved education or training. Blake (16) does not qualify for the claimant because Blake lives with their father who already claims Child Benefit, and the claimant does not contribute financially to Blake's upkeep. Charlie (5) does qualify as they are under 16, live with the claimant in the UK, and nobody else is claiming for them.
